profile.name: Temitayo Charles Akinniranye
profile.title: AI Infrastructure · Platform Engineering · DevOps
profile.tagline: I build production AI infrastructure, model-serving systems, Kubernetes platforms, and operator-friendly automation for teams that need reliable, observable, and scalable engineering foundations.

aboutParagraphs: I build practical infrastructure: Kubernetes platforms, GitOps delivery paths, secure secret workflows, private AI systems, and deployment pipelines that are understandable enough to operate under pressure.

This portfolio is not just a landing page. It is connected to a real Home Lab deployment path, a FastAPI backend, CI checks, container scanning, browser smoke tests, and structured content served through an API.
My focus is useful engineering: clean systems, clear runbooks, reliable recovery paths, and automation that helps operators make better decisions instead of hiding complexity behind dashboards.

aboutHighlights.label: Platform-first
aboutHighlights.value: Kubernetes, GitOps, networking, observability, and recovery designed as one operating model.

aboutHighlights.label: AI with control
aboutHighlights.value: Private assistant workflows with explicit memory, approval gates, and operator ownership.

aboutHighlights.label: Deployment discipline
aboutHighlights.value: Small, reviewable changes backed by CI, smoke tests, image scanning, and rollback-aware delivery.

aboutHighlights.label: Business-aware
aboutHighlights.value: Infrastructure choices explained in terms of reliability, cost, risk, and delivery speed.

experiences.summary: Architect and operate cloud platform, CI/CD, Kubernetes, GitOps, DevSecOps, observability, and reliability workflows for production environments, improving deployment speed, infrastructure reliability, cost governance, and operational consistency.
achievements: Reduced deployment time from 4 hours to 15 minutes via GitHub Actions and Jenkins pipelines.
Enabled 200+ monthly production releases with zero rollbacks through stronger validation and delivery controls.
Lifted platform reliability from 99.9% to 99.99% for infra serving 2M+ API requests / day.
Cut AWS spend by ~$18K / month through audits, rightsizing, tagging, and governance.
Drove GitOps adoption with ArgoCD and Helm, 40+ production releases / week, no weekend windows.
Integrated Trivy, Checkov, SonarQube, and HashiCorp Vault into delivery for DevSecOps and audit readiness.
Designed disaster recovery across 8 services, RTO 4h → 15m, MTTR < 30 minutes.

experiences.summary: Modernized application delivery, cloud infrastructure, automation, monitoring, and operational support for AWS-based environments, supporting cloud migration, microservices delivery, secure infra design, and 24/7 platform operations.
achievements: Led migration of 12 monolithic applications to AWS, 35% infrastructure cost reduction.
Designed GitLab CI/CD workflows scaling releases from weekly to multiple daily pushes.
Built multi-AZ VPCs, least-privilege IAM, and HA EKS clusters with Terraform.
Automated provisioning, patching, and rollouts via Ansible + Terraform, env build days → < 4 hours.
Created Bash / Python automation for backups, restoration, and log management, 40% less manual work.
Established centralized observability with ELK + CloudWatch, 35% lower MTTD on critical incidents.

experiences.summary: Developed and maintained CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure automation, containerized workloads, monitoring systems, and secure AWS operations, strengthening foundations in repeatable delivery and cloud reliability.
achievements: Built CI/CD and infrastructure automation using GitLab CI/CD, Terraform, and Ansible.
Implemented Docker and Kubernetes / EKS practices for scalability and deployment consistency.
Created automated monitoring and logging workflows with ELK Stack and CloudWatch.
Enforced IAM best practices and managed secrets via AWS SSM Parameter Store.

experiences.role: Linux Systems Engineer · Scrum Facilitator · Linux SysAdmin
experiences.company: Beyond Solutions · British Council Bahrain · Cambridge International School
experiences.summary: Managed Linux systems, server operations, backups, monitoring, storage, Apache, MySQL, and infrastructure documentation, building the operational discipline that still shapes how I design reliable platforms today.
achievements: Managed and optimized 200+ Ubuntu and CentOS servers.
Automated backups, log rotation, maintenance, and monitoring with Bash and Python.
Authored technical runbooks and disaster recovery documentation.

projects.subtitle: Production AI inference and model operations laboratory
projects.name: AI Inference Lab
projects.description: A full InfraForge AI lab for LLM inference, model-serving APIs, GPU-backed capacity planning, benchmarking, observability, containerized runtime operations, and the engineering path from local validation to Kubernetes-native AI workloads.
outcomes: Designed the lab as a complete AI infrastructure environment, not a demo: service API, health checks, metrics, container runtime, benchmark harness, documentation, and controlled operating practices.
Established an end-to-end model operations path covering local validation, model loading, latency measurement, concurrency behavior, saturation analysis, and provider-backed GPU expansion.
Mapped AI workload capacity across CPU baselines, GPU experiments, vLLM/Triton-class serving engines, Kubernetes deployment, and InfraForge platform controls.
Built the knowledge system around production signals: p95 latency, throughput, token throughput, TTFT, context windows, KV cache behavior, quantization, cold starts, and model memory sizing.
projects.visibility: Private lab; public portfolio case study

caseStudy.title: AI Inference Lab: InfraForge Model Operations Laboratory
caseStudy.context: A mature InfraForge AI lab built to operate LLM systems with platform-engineering discipline: APIs, runtime controls, metrics, benchmarks, GPU capacity planning, containerization, and a path into Kubernetes-native model operations.
caseStudy.problem: AI infrastructure becomes fragile when it is treated as notebooks and one-off experiments. The lab gives model workloads a production operating model: service boundaries, observability, benchmark evidence, repeatable runtime packaging, GPU capacity strategy, and safe platform controls.
constraints: Support local validation while keeping the system shaped for GPU-backed LLM workloads.
Separate service correctness, runtime performance, and capacity behavior instead of treating a single model response as success.
Control GPU spend through provider selection, budget tiers, and benchmark-driven escalation.
Design the lab so model-serving and training-adjacent workloads can graduate into Kubernetes without losing observability or operational discipline.
decisions: Use FastAPI as the stable service boundary for health, generation, and metrics.
Expose Prometheus-compatible metrics before introducing heavier serving engines.
Treat benchmarks as first-class evidence, covering latency, p95, throughput, concurrency, and saturation.
Use Docker to make the runtime repeatable before scaling the workload.
Run the lab as an InfraForge brand asset: AI systems engineered with the same platform discipline as cloud, DevOps, and SRE workloads.
operationalControls: Health endpoint for service readiness.
Structured API schemas for request and response contracts.
Prometheus request and latency metrics.
Benchmark harness for repeatable load and saturation testing.
Container runtime path for reproducible service packaging.
Environment discovery and GPU provider budget controls.
Documentation system covering concepts, commands, lab phases, validation steps, and operational safety.
evidence: Repository contains FastAPI service implementation, inference module, schemas, logging configuration, Dockerfile, benchmark tooling, and environment validation scripts.

Documentation covers inference fundamentals, inference versus training, CPU versus GPU behavior, latency, throughput, RPS, token throughput, TTFT, batch size, concurrency, KV cache, quantization, context window, cold starts, and model memory sizing.
Benchmark materials include request and concurrency sweeps, p95 latency interpretation, and saturation analysis.
Lab tracker shows the project as a structured engineering program with environment discovery, inference fundamentals, benchmark matrix, and service documentation.

measurementNotes.serviceReadiness: Measured through health endpoint validation and service startup checks.
measurementNotes.latency: Measured through HTTP benchmark output against the generation endpoint, including average and p95 latency.
measurementNotes.throughput: Measured as requests per second across controlled concurrency sweeps.
measurementNotes.capacity: Evaluated through CPU baseline behavior, concurrency saturation, GPU provider planning, and serving-engine comparison.
measurementNotes.operationalMaturity: Demonstrated through docs, benchmark evidence, containerization, metrics, safety rules, and Kubernetes integration planning.

caseStudy.result: The lab presents the AI side of InfraForge as a serious production-capacity system: LLM serving, model operations, observability, benchmarking, GPU capacity planning, and Kubernetes-ready platform design under one operating model.
caseStudy.nextEvolution: Graduate AI workloads from local benchmark validation to GPU-backed Kubernetes inference. Integrate vLLM and Triton serving engines, establish a multi-model capacity model within InfraForge, and extend observability coverage to token throughput, KV cache pressure, and cold-start latency.

projects.name: TCA InfraForge
projects.subtitle: My Platform Engineering Company
projects.description: TCA InfraForge is the platform engineering company I own and operate. It packages Kubernetes operations, GitOps delivery, secrets management, private networking, observability, and recovery workflows into a practical infrastructure model for small teams that need production discipline without building a full platform department.
outcomes: Repeatable platform releases
Clear production cutover path
Recovery-focused operating model

diagramImage.alt: TCA InfraForge company platform architecture diagram

caseStudy.title: How I operate InfraForge as a company platform
caseStudy.context: InfraForge is the business wrapper around my platform engineering work. The goal is to give startups and operators a reliable infrastructure baseline that covers delivery, secrets, routing, observability, backups, recovery, and operational runbooks.
decisions: Use GitOps as the operating source of truth so infrastructure changes are auditable and repeatable.
Separate application delivery from gateway and networking ownership to avoid Argo CD shared-resource drift.
Keep stateful services recoverable through Longhorn retention, Harbor recovery notes, and documented restore paths.
caseStudy.result: The platform now supports production domain routing, Harbor registry recovery, portfolio API delivery, Vault-backed secrets, and a hardened operating model that can be reused for company and client workloads.
caseStudy.problem: Small teams need production discipline across Kubernetes delivery, routing, secrets, observability, and recovery without staffing a full platform department.
constraints: Keep the model small enough for founder-led teams.
Avoid hidden manual steps during cutover and recovery.
Preserve operator visibility across delivery, runtime, and backup layers.
operationalControls: GitOps as source of truth for platform changes.
Vault-backed secrets delivery through External Secrets.
Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, and Alertmanager for runtime visibility.
Longhorn, Harbor notes, and runbook-backed recovery paths.
evidence: Deployment lifecycle measured from approved infrastructure change to reconciled Argo CD application health.
Recovery posture validated through documented registry, storage, and gateway restore procedures.
Platform controls map delivery, secrets, observability, backup, and edge routing into one operating model.

measurementNotes.deploymentCycle: Measured from approved change or merge to production-ready deployment health where the metric applies.
measurementNotes.reliability: Reported as service availability/SLO posture over the operating window where the platform metric applies.
measurementNotes.costSavings: Calculated from recurring monthly AWS spend deltas after rightsizing, tagging, audits, and governance controls.
measurementNotes.recovery: Measured from incident declaration or restore start to verified service recovery where RTO/MTTR applies.

caseStudy.nextEvolution: Expand the platform estate toward multi-cluster federation, harden disaster recovery with tested runbooks under load, and establish the InfraForge public API surface for third-party platform tooling.

projects.name: Project Iris
projects.subtitle: Governed Personal AI Assistant
projects.description: Project Iris is a governed personal AI assistant for everyday digital work: email, calendar, browser workflows, research, memory, artifacts, approvals, tools, MCP, audit, and long-running tasks.
outcomes: Personal work delegation without surrendering user control.
Approval-gated actions for sensitive or consequential tasks.
Memory, audit, and provider boundaries designed into the product from the start.
A practical assistant surface for email, calendar, browser activity, research, artifacts, and follow-up workflows.
projects.repo: Private Project Iris personal AI assistant repository

diagramImage.alt: Project Iris local-first AI workspace architecture diagram

caseStudy.title: Project Iris: governed personal AI for everyday work
caseStudy.context: Iris is a personal AI assistant designed around real daily work: email, calendar, browser tasks, research, memory, artifacts, approvals, and long-running workflows. It is not an engineering assistant or infrastructure dashboard.
caseStudy.problem: People lose time and context switching between email, calendar, research, browser tasks, notes, follow-ups, and decisions. Most assistants either stay too passive to help or become too opaque to trust.
constraints: Keep the assistant personal, not infrastructure-focused.
Make useful delegation possible without hiding important actions from the user.
Treat memory, approvals, provider connections, and audit history as product boundaries, not afterthoughts.
Support everyday workflows while preserving user control over sensitive actions.
decisions: Use approval queues for actions that should not happen silently.
Model memory as governed product infrastructure rather than an invisible black box.
Separate browser, email, calendar, MCP, and provider integrations behind clear contracts.
Use audit and execution workers to make long-running tasks visible and recoverable.
operationalControls: Approval service for governed action flow.
Audit service for traceability.
Memory service for durable personal context.
Provider OAuth boundaries for connected accounts.
MCP gateway for tool access through explicit contracts.
Execution worker for long-running task orchestration.
evidence: Repository contains API and UI surfaces for approvals, audit, memory, provider connections, tools, and operator workflows.
Documentation identifies Iris as a personal AI operator assistant rather than an engineering dashboard.
Service structure includes approval, audit, memory, browser, email, calendar, MCP gateway, notification, and execution-worker concerns.
caseStudy.result: Iris demonstrates a premium personal AI pattern: capable enough to help with real work, but governed enough to preserve user control, memory boundaries, auditability, and trust.
caseStudy.nextEvolution: Move Iris from local MCP gateway to a hosted personal AI service with cloud-backed memory and user-approved retention policies. Expand tool coverage to project tracking, financial dashboards, and meeting workflows.

projects.name: Jerry
projects.subtitle: Mobile AI Interaction Layer
projects.description: Jerry is the mobile AI companion for quick commands, status checks, alert review, approvals, and lightweight assistant interaction. It gives personal and operational AI workflows a mobile surface without pretending to replace Iris or ForgeWatch.
outcomes: Lightweight mobile access to AI interaction and assistant workflows.
Command policy and health checks that make the assistant runtime more governable.
A companion surface for quick status, alerts, approvals, and mobile-first interaction.
A bridge between personal AI interaction and operational awareness without collapsing those products into one.
projects.repo: Private Jerry / AI Mobile Chat repository

caseStudy.title: Jerry: the mobile companion for governed AI interaction
caseStudy.context: Jerry gives the InfraForge ecosystem a lightweight mobile AI companion for quick commands, status checks, approvals, alert review, and assistant interaction.
caseStudy.problem: Useful AI assistance often stays locked in desktop workflows. People need fast, mobile access to status, commands, approvals, and lightweight assistant interaction while they are away from a workstation.
constraints: Keep Jerry lightweight and mobile-first.
Do not position Jerry as the personal AI brain; that role belongs to Iris.
Do not position Jerry as the infrastructure watcher; that role belongs to ForgeWatch.
Keep command behavior policy-aware and observable.
decisions: Use command policy to make allowed actions explicit.
Keep health checks close to the assistant runtime.
Treat mobile interaction as a companion layer for personal and operational AI workflows.
Preserve separation between Jerry, Iris, ForgeWatch, and InfraForge Platform.
operationalControls: Command policy file.
Assistant runtime health check.
Agent instruction boundary.
Lightweight mobile interaction surface.
evidence: Repository includes AGENTS.md, command policy, runtime app, health-check script, and dependency manifest.
Project structure supports a policy-aware mobile AI assistant pattern rather than a generic chatbot.
caseStudy.result: Jerry makes AI interaction more available and responsive by bringing quick commands, status, approvals, and assistant interaction into a mobile-first companion surface.
caseStudy.nextEvolution: Extend Jerry's command surface to cover calendar management, project tracking, and financial dashboards. Add persistent notification paths and a structured daily briefing format for mobile-first workflows.

projects.name: AI Builders Academy
projects.subtitle: Practical AI Builders Learning Platform
projects.description: AI Builders Academy turns AI curiosity into structured learning experiences that connect concepts to product-building practice, platform delivery, and responsible AI implementation.
outcomes: Structured path from AI curiosity to practical AI product building.
Learning platform that connects AI concepts to real implementation patterns.
Delivery discipline for an education product that can evolve beyond prototype stage.

caseStudy.title: AI Builders Academy: practical AI education for builders
caseStudy.context: AI Builders Academy packages AI and platform knowledge into guided learning experiences for people who want to build real AI-enabled systems, not just consume AI theory.
decisions: Refine deployable path filters so only relevant changes trigger product delivery workflows.
Promote runtime images deliberately instead of relying on ambiguous moving state.
Add drift checks and runbooks so platform behavior stays visible after deploy.
caseStudy.result: The platform extends InfraForge into AI education: practical, product-minded, and connected to the same systems discipline behind the rest of the ecosystem.

projects.name: Vault Ops Toolkit
projects.subtitle: Secrets Operations for Platform Teams
projects.description: Vault Ops Toolkit helps operators handle secrets safely across environments with Vault-backed workflows, Kubernetes External Secrets validation, bootstrap automation, and practical recovery procedures.
outcomes: Secret recovery workflows
ExternalSecret validation
Operator-safe automation

caseStudy.title: Secrets operations that teams can safely run
caseStudy.context: Vault Ops exists for platform teams that need secure secret workflows without turning every rotation, sync, or recovery step into risky manual work.
decisions: Keep Vault as the authority while External Secrets handles Kubernetes delivery.
Document bootstrap and recovery paths so operators can act under pressure.
Validate secret shape and sync state before application rollouts depend on them.
caseStudy.result: The toolkit gives platform work a safer secrets operating model that is audit-friendly and practical for small teams.
caseStudy.problem: Platform teams need safe, repeatable secret operations across Vault and Kubernetes without relying on fragile manual steps.
constraints: Keep Vault as the authority.
Validate Kubernetes secret sync before application rollout.
Document recovery and rollback for operators working under pressure.
operationalControls: Vault-backed secret authority.
External Secrets sync validation.
Pre-rollout secret shape checks.
Documented rotation and rollback runbook.
evidence: Secret workflows separate authority, sync, validation, and application dependency checks.
Runbook path covers rotation, ExternalSecret verification, rollout safety, and rollback.
Operator controls are designed for repeatability under incident pressure.

measurementNotes.deploymentCycle: Measured from approved change or merge to production-ready deployment health where the metric applies.
measurementNotes.reliability: Reported as service availability/SLO posture over the operating window where the platform metric applies.
measurementNotes.costSavings: Calculated from recurring monthly AWS spend deltas after rightsizing, tagging, audits, and governance controls.
measurementNotes.recovery: Measured from incident declaration or restore start to verified service recovery where RTO/MTTR applies.

caseStudy.nextEvolution: Automate the full secret rotation lifecycle end-to-end with CI/CD pre-deploy gate integration. Publish runbook templates as an open-source operator toolkit for Vault and Kubernetes platform teams.

projects.name: ForgeWatch
projects.subtitle: Evidence-First AIOps Copilot for InfraForge
projects.description: ForgeWatch is the operational intelligence and cluster guardian layer for InfraForge. It watches infrastructure signals, KeepHQ events, Kubernetes state, alerts, incidents, and service health, then helps operators understand what needs attention.
outcomes: Infrastructure signals connected into a clearer operational picture.
Evidence-first incident review across metrics, Kubernetes state, and service health.
Human-approved remediation path that keeps operators in control.
projects.visibility: Private lab; portfolio case study available

caseStudy.title: Evidence-first operations for lean platform teams
caseStudy.context: ForgeWatch is the operational intelligence layer and cluster guardian for InfraForge. It watches metrics, incidents, alerts, KeepHQ signals, Kubernetes state, and service health, then turns operational noise into structured understanding.
decisions: Keep Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD queries autonomous but read-only by default.
Route all mutating actions through propose_write, policy evaluation, and explicit operator approval.
Store outcomes and incident memory so future triage improves without turning the system into an unsafe autonomous executor.
caseStudy.result: ForgeWatch gives InfraForge an operational intelligence layer where AI helps connect signals and speed investigation, while operators retain authority over production state.
caseStudy.problem: Infrastructure teams are overwhelmed by scattered alerts, metrics, incidents, Kubernetes state, logs, and service-health signals.
constraints: Do not allow uncontrolled production writes.
Ground every recommendation in Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, or Argo CD evidence.
Keep human approval mandatory for remediation.
operationalControls: Read-only Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD collection.
Approval-gated write proposals.
Outcome-backed memory for future triage.
Notification path through Discord and ntfy.
evidence: Investigation tools stay read-only by default.
Write actions require proposal, policy check, and explicit operator approval.
Incident memory stores outcomes rather than blindly executing remediation.

measurementNotes.deploymentCycle: Measured from approved change or merge to production-ready deployment health where the metric applies.
measurementNotes.reliability: Reported as service availability/SLO posture over the operating window where the platform metric applies.
measurementNotes.costSavings: Calculated from recurring monthly AWS spend deltas after rightsizing, tagging, audits, and governance controls.
measurementNotes.recovery: Measured from incident declaration or restore start to verified service recovery where RTO/MTTR applies.

caseStudy.nextEvolution: Move ForgeWatch toward autonomous triage workflows with graduated approval thresholds. Expand the RAG memory corpus with incident history and resolution outcomes. Add cross-cluster observability and drift-detection alerting.

projects.name: OpenLeaf Reader Platform

projects.visibility: Private lab; portfolio case study available

caseStudy.title: A SaaS architecture reference for digital products
caseStudy.context: OpenLeaf models the architecture patterns a digital content business needs once product delivery moves beyond a simple monolith.
decisions: Use gateway governance to keep APIs discoverable and controlled.
Model event-driven boundaries where services need asynchronous coordination.
Include observability and chaos testing so reliability is visible, not assumed.
caseStudy.result: The project serves as a public architecture reference for product teams thinking through cloud-native SaaS design.
caseStudy.problem: Digital product teams need a reference SaaS architecture that shows service boundaries, API governance, delivery, observability, and reliability controls together.
constraints: Avoid a toy monolith while keeping the reference understandable.
Show how gateway, events, payments, and observability relate.
Make reliability testing part of the architecture, not an afterthought.
operationalControls: Kong and decK for gateway governance.
RabbitMQ for asynchronous service coordination.
Prometheus and Chaos Mesh for reliability visibility.
Argo CD delivery path for platform-managed releases.
evidence: Architecture models gateway governance, evented boundaries, payment workflows, and reliability testing.
Public repository provides implementation-level inspection for the reference SaaS design.
Release model connects frontend, API, service, and platform concerns.

measurementNotes.deploymentCycle: Measured from approved change or merge to production-ready deployment health where the metric applies.
measurementNotes.reliability: Reported as service availability/SLO posture over the operating window where the platform metric applies.
measurementNotes.costSavings: Calculated from recurring monthly AWS spend deltas after rightsizing, tagging, audits, and governance controls.
measurementNotes.recovery: Measured from incident declaration or restore start to verified service recovery where RTO/MTTR applies.

caseStudy.nextEvolution: Evolve OpenLeaf into a deployable SaaS reference kit with real tenant isolation, validated chaos engineering results, and publicly available architecture documentation for platform engineering teams.

projectArchitectures.label: Company platform
projectArchitectures.title: TCA InfraForge operating platform
projectArchitectures.summary: The company platform that packages Kubernetes delivery, GitOps, secrets, private networking, observability, registry recovery, and disaster recovery into a repeatable operating system for startups and growing teams.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows that InfraForge is a real platform company with an owned operating model, not a collection of lab resources.
projectArchitectures.metric: GitOps, secrets, registry, DR, and edge routing

nodes.label: Cloudflare
nodes.details: Public DNS, TLS edge, caching, and routed entry for company and client-facing endpoints.

nodes.label: Tailscale
nodes.details: Private operator access path for internal systems and administrative workflows.

nodes.label: Envoy Gateway
nodes.details: Gateway API routing for internal products, Harbor, portfolio API, and platform services.

nodes.label: Argo CD
nodes.details: GitOps reconciler that applies platform applications, sync waves, and ownership boundaries.

nodes.label: Vault
nodes.details: Secrets authority for platform and workload credentials.

nodes.label: External Secrets
nodes.details: Kubernetes bridge that syncs approved secret values into application namespaces.

nodes.label: Workloads
nodes.details: Portfolio API, AI Builders, ForgeWatch, platform services, and client-ready workloads.

nodes.label: Harbor
nodes.details: Private container registry with retained Longhorn storage and recovery runbooks.

nodes.label: Longhorn
nodes.details: Persistent volume layer for registry, databases, and other stateful services.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.details: Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, Alertmanager, Netdata, and runbooks for operational visibility.

flows: Git commit updates the platform repository, then Argo CD reconciles the desired state into the cluster.
Cloudflare sends public traffic to Envoy Gateway, then Gateway API routes requests to the correct namespace service.
Vault remains the source of truth for credentials while External Secrets delivers only approved runtime values.
Longhorn retained volumes and runbooks make stateful recovery practical after namespace or pod failure.

projectArchitectures.label: Private AI workspace
projectArchitectures.title: Project Iris governed AI workspace
projectArchitectures.summary: A local-first AI workspace for founders and operators that treats memory, browser actions, tool use, TLS, backup, and identity as governed infrastructure.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Positions private AI as a controlled product workspace for teams that need capability without losing custody of data and actions.
projectArchitectures.metric: Private memory, tool control, and recovery

nodes.label: Operator
nodes.details: Founder, operator, or technical user working inside a private AI workspace.

nodes.label: React UI
nodes.details: Workspace interface for conversations, workflows, managed actions, and project context.

nodes.label: FastAPI
nodes.details: Backend boundary for auth, memory operations, tool routing, and workflow state.

nodes.label: Auth + TLS
nodes.details: Identity and transport controls so the workspace is not an open local demo.

nodes.label: Memory Store
nodes.details: Governed long-term memory with explicit user control and backup awareness.

nodes.label: Tool Router
nodes.details: Explicit tool execution boundary for browser actions, local actions, and workflow helpers.

nodes.label: Managed Browser
nodes.details: Controlled browser action path for interactive tasks that should remain visible and governable.

nodes.label: Backups
nodes.details: Backup and restore path for workspace state, memory, and configuration.

flows: The user interacts through a private UI, not an uncontrolled public assistant surface.
The API checks auth and routes memory or tool requests through explicit backend boundaries.
Tool execution remains visible and separated from memory so users understand what the assistant can do.
Backups protect workspace state so the system can be recovered after local or runtime failure.

projectArchitectures.label: Personal AI system
projectArchitectures.title: Jerry durable personal assistant architecture
projectArchitectures.summary: Jerry is modeled as a local-first personal assistant with durable memory, retrieval, guarded actions, and backup-aware state. The architecture frames the assistant as an owned operating system for personal productivity rather than a novelty chatbot.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows how personal AI can be designed with the same discipline as production software: memory, consent, auditability, durability, and reversible execution.
projectArchitectures.metric: Durable memory, local-first control, guarded execution

nodes.label: Input Channels
nodes.details: Chat, notes, local files, browser context, images, and other user-controlled sources.

nodes.label: Assistant Core
nodes.details: Intent routing, task continuity, conversation state, reminders, and personal workflow coordination.

nodes.label: Local Models
nodes.details: Local model runtime and embeddings support private reasoning, retrieval, and summarization.

nodes.label: Semantic Memory
nodes.details: Durable memory treats personal context as governed infrastructure rather than transient chat history.

nodes.label: Guarded Actions
nodes.details: File operations, browser tasks, notifications, and sync flows pass through consent-aware execution boundaries.

nodes.label: SQLite + Backups
nodes.details: Primary local state with backup and replication paths for continuity after device or runtime failure.

nodes.label: Audit Trail
nodes.details: Actions remain reviewable and reversible so the assistant earns trust through visibility.

flows: User context enters through explicit channels so the assistant does not blur private sources with uncontrolled automation.
The assistant core routes intent through local models, semantic memory, and task continuity before recommending or taking action.
Any meaningful action crosses a guarded execution boundary with consent, logging, and reviewable outcomes.
SQLite and backup paths make memory durable enough to support long-running personal workflows.

projectArchitectures.label: Education business platform
projectArchitectures.title: AI Builders Academy delivery system
projectArchitectures.summary: A business delivery platform for AI education, with app changes, image promotion, GitOps, runtime checks, and operational runbooks tied into one product release lane.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows how an education business can move quickly while keeping deployable boundaries, runtime state, and platform ownership visible.
projectArchitectures.metric: Deployable paths, image promotion, drift checks

nodes.label: Program Content
nodes.details: Curriculum, program assets, and delivery material for AI education products.

nodes.label: Frontend
nodes.details: Student and buyer-facing application interface.

nodes.label: Backend API
nodes.details: Business logic, enrollment workflows, admin operations, and product APIs.

nodes.label: CI Path Filters
nodes.details: Refined deployable path filters prevent unrelated changes from triggering unsafe releases.

nodes.label: Image Registry
nodes.details: Runtime images are promoted deliberately instead of relying on ambiguous state.

nodes.label: GitOps Release
nodes.details: Argo CD applies the promoted release into the target environment.

nodes.label: Runtime Checks
nodes.details: Drift checks and smoke validation keep deployed behavior visible.

nodes.label: Runbooks
nodes.details: Operational notes describe recovery, deployment, and verification procedures.

flows: Relevant code paths trigger the correct build lane and avoid noisy unrelated deployment activity.
Images are built, promoted, and reconciled through GitOps so release state is reviewable.
Runtime drift checks verify that the deployed product matches the expected operating state.
Runbooks document recovery and validation so the business can keep operating after changes.

projectArchitectures.label: Secrets operations
projectArchitectures.title: Vault Ops secrets control plane
projectArchitectures.summary: A secrets operations toolkit for Vault-backed platform teams that need bootstrap, rotation, External Secrets sync, validation, and recovery procedures.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Turns secrets management into a runbook-backed platform capability that teams can operate safely under pressure.

nodes.label: Platform Operator
nodes.details: Authorized operator who bootstraps, validates, and rotates platform secrets.

nodes.label: Vault
nodes.details: System of record for sensitive credentials and environment values.

nodes.label: Policies
nodes.details: Access policy and path structure that defines who can read or write secret material.

nodes.label: External Secrets
nodes.details: Controller that syncs selected Vault values into Kubernetes secrets.

nodes.label: K8s Secrets
nodes.details: Namespace-local runtime secret objects consumed by workloads.

nodes.label: Workloads
nodes.details: Applications that consume secrets without direct Vault coupling.

nodes.label: Validation
nodes.details: Shape checks, sync checks, and recovery evidence before rollout dependency.

flows: Operators place credentials in Vault under known paths and policy boundaries.
External Secrets syncs approved values to Kubernetes namespaces without exposing Vault broadly.
Workloads consume Kubernetes secrets while audit checks confirm the secret shape before rollout.
Recovery procedures restore missing or broken secrets from the authority source instead of guessing values.

projectArchitectures.label: AIOps copilot
projectArchitectures.title: ForgeWatch evidence-first operations copilot
projectArchitectures.summary: ForgeWatch uses Ollama function calling to gather read-only evidence from Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD. It can propose write actions, but policy and operator approval control every mutation.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Gives InfraForge and lean platform teams a safer AIOps loop where AI accelerates diagnosis without silently changing production.

nodes.label: Operator
nodes.details: Engineer or platform owner asking questions, reviewing evidence, and approving or denying proposed actions.

nodes.label: ForgeWatch API
nodes.details: FastAPI service exposing ask, alert, digest, watchdog, outcome, memory, and writes routes.

nodes.label: Ollama Loop
nodes.details: Six-round tool-calling loop that decides which evidence sources to query before answering.

nodes.label: Tool Executor
nodes.details: Runs read-only tools immediately and gates write proposals through policy and approval.

nodes.label: Evidence Sources
nodes.details: Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD clients provide real-time platform evidence.

nodes.label: RAG Memory
nodes.details: ChromaDB, outcomes, history, and runbooks improve future triage with confirmed context.

nodes.label: Policy Engine
nodes.details: Classifies write intent, blocks dangerous commands, and forces approval for mutating actions.

nodes.label: Discord / ntfy
nodes.details: Optional proactive notifications for digest, watchdog, and startup status.

flows: An operator or alert calls the API with a platform question or incident payload.
Ollama selects tools and the executor queries Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, or Argo CD for evidence.
If remediation is needed, ForgeWatch creates a write proposal instead of executing it automatically.
Policy evaluates the command, then the operator approves or denies through the writes endpoint.
Outcomes and incident notes feed memory so future responses improve with confirmed operational context.

projectArchitectures.label: SaaS reference
projectArchitectures.title: OpenLeaf digital product platform
projectArchitectures.summary: A reference SaaS architecture for digital content products with API gateway governance, service boundaries, async messaging, payments, observability, GitOps, and reliability testing.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows how a digital product company can evolve from app code into service ownership, gateway control, and reliability practice.

nodes.label: Reader UI
nodes.details: Customer-facing reading and content experience.

nodes.label: Kong Gateway
nodes.details: API gateway governance with route, policy, and service exposure control.

nodes.label: User Service
nodes.details: Identity and user profile boundary.

nodes.label: Book Service
nodes.details: Content catalog and reader domain boundary.

nodes.label: Payments
nodes.details: Stripe or payment provider integration for subscriptions and transactions.

nodes.label: RabbitMQ
nodes.details: Asynchronous coordination for product events and service communication.

nodes.label: MongoDB
nodes.details: Document data store for reader and product state.

nodes.label: Prometheus + Chaos
nodes.details: Metrics and reliability testing make service behavior visible under stress.

flows: Readers use the frontend, while Kong keeps service APIs governed through one controlled entry point.
User, catalog, and billing services own separate business boundaries rather than one tangled app surface.
RabbitMQ carries async events so product actions can be processed without coupling every service directly.
Prometheus and chaos testing expose reliability behavior before customers experience hidden failure modes.

writings.title: Building the InfraForge AI Inference Lab
writings.excerpt: A production AI lab is not a notebook. It needs model-serving APIs, benchmark evidence, Prometheus metrics, containerized runtime controls, GPU capacity strategy, and a path into Kubernetes-native LLM operations.
writings.noteBody: The AI side of my work is about operating model workloads properly: runtime boundaries, health checks, metrics, p95 latency, concurrency, GPU capacity, containerization, and safe progression into Kubernetes.
writings.detailSummary: This piece outlines why AI Inference Lab is operated as platform infrastructure with measurable runtime behavior and disciplined rollout patterns.

bodySections.title: Operational baseline
bodySections.body: Serving APIs, benchmark evidence, and metrics are treated as release prerequisites, not optional add-ons.

bodySections.title: Scaling posture
bodySections.body: The article explains a practical progression from local serving to containerized workloads and Kubernetes-aligned operations.

takeaways: Model operations must be observable to be trusted.
Latency and throughput evidence are core product signals.
AI runtime quality depends on platform discipline.

writings.title: Cutting a 4-Hour Deploy to 15 Minutes: A GitOps Playbook
writings.excerpt: How we replaced a brittle weekly release train with ArgoCD, reusable Helm charts, and automated release validation. Covers reusable pipeline primitives, rollout strategy, and the quality gates that let us ship 40+ production releases per week.
writings.noteTitle: How I design Kubernetes platforms for companies
writings.noteBody: The goal is not to collect Kubernetes tools. The goal is to give a company a stable operating model: Git as the source of truth, a gateway layer that owns ingress clearly, a secrets path that operators can audit, observability that explains failures, and recovery procedures that are tested before they are needed.
writings.detailSummary: A practical route from fragile release routines to GitOps-driven delivery with explicit quality gates and repeatable rollback posture.

bodySections.title: Delivery controls
bodySections.body: The piece emphasizes source-of-truth discipline, Helm/Kubernetes repeatability, and pre-merge validation as stability drivers.

bodySections.title: Team impact
bodySections.body: Release confidence improves when workflow ownership, environment parity, and rollback expectations are explicit.

takeaways: GitOps is an operating model, not a tooling checklist.
Release speed and safety should rise together.
Validation gates reduce late-stage production surprises.

writings.title: Shaving $216K a Year Off AWS Without Breaking Production
writings.excerpt: A practical FinOps walkthrough: tagging strategy, rightsizing cadence, savings-plan bets, idle compute hunting, and how governance prevented regressions at 2M+ API requests per day.
writings.noteBody: AI operations should not mean blind automation. I design the flow so telemetry, logs, runbooks, and prior incidents create context first. The assistant proposes ranked actions, but the operator keeps approval authority. That keeps AI useful without giving it unsafe production control.
writings.detailSummary: A cost-governance pattern focused on sustainable savings while preserving reliability and production confidence.

bodySections.title: Governance model
bodySections.body: The article presents tagging discipline, rightsizing review cycles, and spend visibility as recurring platform practices.

bodySections.title: Reliability guardrails
bodySections.body: Optimization decisions remain bounded by service performance and operational risk controls.

takeaways: FinOps works best as a continuous engineering loop.
Savings should never be detached from reliability goals.
Cost controls are strongest when embedded in platform routines.

writings.title: Designing AIOps with Human-in-the-Loop Safety
writings.excerpt: Why evidence-grounded triage, runbook memory, and read-only analysis are the default posture before you let LLMs near production. A deep dive into the ForgeWatch approach.
writings.noteBody: GitOps works best when teams treat it as a control system, not just a deploy button. Ownership boundaries, sync waves, rollback paths, and drift checks matter because they prevent platform teams from debugging invisible state during outages.
writings.detailSummary: An evidence-first AIOps framing where automation quality is gated by observability quality and human approval boundaries.

bodySections.title: Evidence before action
bodySections.body: The writing highlights read-first diagnostics and context synthesis before any remediation proposal is considered.

bodySections.title: Governance boundary
bodySections.body: Human approval remains explicit to keep operational authority clear during incidents.

takeaways: AIOps credibility starts with signal quality.
Approval gates are a feature, not friction.
Operator trust is earned through transparent decision flow.

writings.title: 99.99% Isn't a Metric, It's a Culture
writings.excerpt: From error budgets and SLOs to on-call hygiene and blameless postmortems. How platform discipline, not heroics, gets you the extra nine.
writings.noteBody: Secrets work should be boring, auditable, and recoverable. Vault, External Secrets, and clear runbooks let a team rotate credentials, recover missing values, and validate application configuration without guessing under pressure.
writings.detailSummary: A reliability narrative focused on operational culture, incident learning, and measurable service posture rather than slogan metrics.

bodySections.title: Reliability loop
bodySections.body: The article links SLOs, on-call hygiene, and post-incident review quality into a single operational feedback system.

bodySections.title: Execution habits
bodySections.body: It emphasizes repeatable runbooks, alert quality, and recovery rehearsal as practical reliability multipliers.

takeaways: Reliability is produced by habits, not heroics.
Error budgets help align engineering tradeoffs.
Postmortems are strongest when action-oriented.

writings.title: Bare-Metal Kubernetes That Feels Like Production
writings.excerpt: Inside TCA InfraForge: Ubuntu + K3s, Gateway API via Envoy, Longhorn storage, External Secrets with Vault, Velero backups, and a Kyverno policy stack that mirrors enterprise controls.
writings.noteBody: Recovery is only real when operators know which data matters, where it lives, how to restore it, and how to prove the application works after the restore. For stateful Kubernetes services, volume retention, PVC binding, and app-level validation all matter.
writings.detailSummary: A production-discipline look at bare-metal Kubernetes that prioritizes governance, recoverability, and platform ownership.

bodySections.title: Platform composition
bodySections.body: The article maps ingress, storage, policy, and secret workflows into a coherent operational baseline.

bodySections.title: Recovery realism
bodySections.body: It treats backup and restore pathways as day-one engineering requirements rather than afterthoughts.

takeaways: Bare-metal can be production-grade with disciplined controls.
Policy and observability should be built in early.
Recovery verification is part of platform readiness.

writings.title: Supply Chain Security on a Delivery Team's Budget
writings.excerpt: How to fold Trivy, Checkov, SonarQube, and Vault-backed secrets into an existing CI/CD pipeline without slowing down the team or turning security into a bottleneck.
writings.noteTitle: Supply chain security without blocking delivery
writings.noteBody: Security checks become useful when they are placed where developers already work. Trivy, Checkov, SonarQube, and Vault-backed secrets can improve confidence without becoming release theater if findings are visible, prioritized, and connected to action.
writings.detailSummary: A practical approach to supply-chain hardening that keeps security actionable inside daily delivery workflows.

bodySections.title: Pipeline integration
bodySections.body: The piece explains placing scans and policy checks where developers already work so feedback is timely and usable.

bodySections.title: Adoption posture
bodySections.body: It argues for prioritized findings and operational follow-through instead of compliance theater.

takeaways: Security controls should reinforce, not block, delivery.
Visibility without prioritization creates noise.
Sustained hardening depends on workflow fit.

testimonials.name: Adaeze O.
testimonials.quote: Temitayo rebuilt our delivery pipeline from a brittle weekly release into a 40-per-week GitOps engine. The team finally trusts production again, and our weekends came back.

testimonials.name: Marcus L.
testimonials.quote: He cut our AWS spend by ~$18K/month in the first quarter without a single performance regression. Rare to find an engineer who blends FinOps discipline with deep platform skills.

testimonials.name: Priya S.
testimonials.quote: Our MTTR dropped under 30 minutes after Temitayo redesigned the observability stack. Runbooks became real, alerts became actionable, and incident reviews became evidence-driven.

testimonials.name: Daniel K.
testimonials.quote: The bare-metal Kubernetes platform he designed for us reads like a textbook on production discipline, GitOps, Vault, External Secrets, Velero, Kyverno, every layer thought through.

testimonials.name: Sarah M.
testimonials.quote: Temitayo is the rare engineer who can ship reliable infrastructure and explain it to auditors, executives, and junior engineers in the same week. SOC 2 evidence packets practically wrote themselves.

testimonials.name: Jonas R.
testimonials.quote: His AI-assisted ops workflow gave us evidence-grounded triage with human approval gates baked in, exactly the safety posture we needed before letting LLMs near production.

brandSystems.thesis: InfraForge builds governed AI systems and the infrastructure they need to operate reliably.
brandSystems.publicHubRole: temitayocharles.online is the public InfraForge brand hub. It explains the ecosystem; it is not a node inside the ecosystem map.
narrativeRules: Tell problem-solution stories instead of exposing internal architecture as a diagram dump.
Keep Iris positioned as a governed personal AI assistant, not an engineering assistant.
Keep Jerry positioned as a mobile AI companion and interaction surface.
Keep ForgeWatch positioned as operational intelligence and cluster guardian.
Keep private repositories represented as private proof assets, not broken public links.
Avoid generic blue AI/SaaS visual language.

systems.name: InfraForge Platform
systems.problem: Modern AI and software products need reliable infrastructure, delivery, secrets, observability, and automation from the beginning.
systems.solution: InfraForge Platform provides the operating foundation for clusters, GitOps delivery, Vault-backed secrets, observability, CI/CD, runbooks, and repeatable deployment control.

systems.name: AI Inference Lab
systems.problem: AI models are easy to demo but hard to operate, measure, package, and scale reliably.
systems.solution: AI Inference Lab turns model serving into an observable, benchmarked, capacity-aware discipline with runtime packaging, metrics, latency/throughput tracking, and GPU-capacity planning.

systems.name: ForgeWatch / Sentinel Copilot
systems.problem: Infrastructure teams are overwhelmed by scattered alerts, metrics, incidents, Kubernetes state, logs, and service-health signals.
systems.solution: ForgeWatch watches the operational estate, connects signals through systems such as KeepHQ, classifies operational events, and helps operators understand what needs attention.

systems.name: Project Iris
systems.problem: People lose time switching between email, calendar, browser tasks, research, memory, documents, follow-ups, and decisions.
systems.solution: Iris helps manage everyday digital work through governed AI delegation: memory, approvals, tools, MCP, email, calendar, browser workflows, artifacts, audit, and long-running tasks.

systems.name: Jerry / AI Mobile Chat
systems.role: Mobile AI companion and interaction surface
systems.problem: AI assistance is often trapped in desktop workflows when people need quick mobile interaction, status, commands, and approval-style actions.
systems.solution: Jerry provides a lightweight mobile AI companion for commands, status checks, assistant interaction, alert review, and mobile access to governed AI workflows.

systems.name: Young Coders
systems.problem: Young learners need structured, engaging, practical paths into coding that feel real, not abstract.
systems.solution: Young Coders turns coding education into a structured product experience with learner flows, exercises, platform scaffolding, and production-minded delivery.

systems.name: AI Builders Academy
systems.problem: Learners need a practical path from AI curiosity to building real AI-enabled systems.
systems.solution: AI Builders Academy packages AI and platform knowledge into guided learning experiences that connect concepts to product-building practice.

systems.name: Vault Ops
systems.problem: Secrets operations become risky when they rely on manual, undocumented, or unvalidated changes.
systems.solution: Vault Ops turns secret handling into a governed, repeatable, auditable process across Vault, Kubernetes, External Secrets, validation, rollout, and rollback.

relationshipModel: InfraForge Platform provides the secure runtime foundation.
AI Inference Lab proves model operations and LLM-serving capacity.
ForgeWatch watches infrastructure and operational signals.
Iris manages personal digital work through governed AI delegation.
Jerry provides mobile access to lightweight AI interaction and status/approval flows.
Young Coders and AI Builders Academy productize the knowledge layer.
Vault Ops strengthens the security and secrets story.
